howItWorks.intro: Baker's percentage expresses every ingredient as a percentage of total flour weight. Flour is always 100%, and all other ingredients are relative to it. This system makes scaling recipes and comparing formulas easy.
rules: Flour is always 100% — it's the base for all calculations
To scale: multiply each percentage by the new flour weight and divide by 100
Hydration = water ÷ flour × 100 (the most important ratio in bread baking)
howItWorks.outro: Baker's percentage is the universal language of professional bakers. Once you know a formula's percentages, you can scale it to any batch size — from a single loaf to a commercial bakery run.

faq.question: What is baker's percentage?
faq.answer: Baker's percentage is a system where every ingredient weight is expressed as a percentage of total flour weight. Flour is always 100%. If you have 1000g flour and 650g water, the water is 65%. This makes it easy to scale recipes and compare bread formulas.

faq.question: Why is flour always 100%?
faq.answer: Flour is the foundation of bread. By making it 100%, all other ratios stay constant regardless of batch size. Whether you use 500g or 5kg of flour, 65% hydration always means 65g water per 100g flour.

faq.question: What is a good hydration percentage for bread?
faq.answer: Standard sandwich bread is 60-65% hydration. Artisan bread like ciabatta or sourdough ranges from 65-80%. Focaccia can go up to 80-90%. Higher hydration produces more open crumb but is harder to handle.

faq.question: How much salt should bread have?
faq.answer: Most bread formulas use 1.8-2.2% salt (baker's percentage). That's 18-22g per 1000g flour. Salt controls fermentation speed, strengthens gluten, and adds flavor. Going below 1.5% produces bland bread.

faq.question: How do I scale a recipe using baker's percentage?
faq.answer: Multiply your target flour weight by each ingredient's percentage and divide by 100. For example, if your formula is 65% water and you want 750g flour: 750 × 65 ÷ 100 = 487.5g water.

faq.question: Does baker's percentage work for sourdough starter?
faq.answer: Yes, but note that sourdough starter contains both flour and water. A 100% hydration starter is 50% flour and 50% water. For precise calculations, split your starter weight and add its flour and water to the respective totals.

sections.title: What Is the Baker's Percentage Calculator?
sections.body: The Baker's Percentage Calculator is a free online tool designed for home cooks and chefs who need quick, accurate calculations in the cooking and food preparation space. By entering your total flour weight, water weight, salt weight, you get instant results including hydration, total dough weight. No formulas to memorize, no spreadsheets to build — just enter your numbers and get the answer in seconds. Whether you're a beginner or experienced professional, this calculator saves you time and eliminates guesswork.

sections.title: Why This Calculation Matters
sections.body: Getting hydration right can make the difference between success and costly mistakes. In cooking and food preparation, small errors compound quickly. Manual calculations are error-prone and time-consuming, especially under pressure. This calculator applies proven formulas used by home cooks and chefs worldwide, giving you confidence that your numbers are correct. Use it to perfect your recipes with precision and avoid common pitfalls that trip up beginners.

sections.title: When Should You Use This Calculator?
sections.body: This tool is most useful when you know your total flour weight and need to find the right hydration. It's also great for quick estimates before committing to a decision, and to double-check manual calculations or professional quotes, and when comparing different scenarios side by side. Bookmark this page and come back whenever you need a fast, reliable answer — the calculator is always free and requires no signup.

howToSteps.title: Enter Your Total Flour Weight (g)
howToSteps.description: Start by entering your total flour weight — this is the primary input for the calculation.

howToSteps.title: Fill In Additional Details
howToSteps.description: Complete the remaining fields: water weight, salt weight, yeast / starter weight, sugar / sweetener — optional, fat / oil / butter — optional, scale to new flour weight — optional. Each value refines the calculation for greater accuracy.

howToSteps.title: Click Calculate
howToSteps.description: Hit the Calculate button to run the numbers. Results appear instantly below.

howToSteps.title: Review Your Results
howToSteps.description: Check your hydration, total dough weight. Use these figures to inform your next decision or compare against alternative scenarios.

tips: Double-check your total flour weight before calculating — even small input errors can significantly change your results.
Run the calculator with different values to compare scenarios and find the optimal approach for your situation.
Pay attention to both hydration and total dough weight — they work together to give you the full picture.
Bookmark this page for quick access next time you need to perfect your recipes.
If you're unsure about your scale to new flour weight — optional, start with a conservative estimate and adjust from there.
